event: View of Le Puy Cathedral facade
symbols: cathedral facade with ascending stairs — the ascent to the house of God, the pilgrim's journey upward
polychrome stonework — the diversity and richness of creation offered back to God in worship
triple portals — the entrance to the sacred, recalling the Trinity
description: A photograph of the imposing western facade of Le Puy Cathedral in France, viewed from below looking up a broad stone staircase. The Romanesque facade features multiple tiers of arched openings, with three large arched portals at ground level. The stonework is distinctively polychrome, with alternating bands of light and dark stone creating geometric patterns. Triangular gables crown the upper level. People are visible on the steps for scale, showing the massive size of the building. The sky is overcast.
theological_description: Le Puy Cathedral is one of the great pilgrimage churches of France, a starting point for the Way of St. James (Camino de Santiago). The dramatic staircase ascending to the facade embodies Psalm 122:1, 'I was glad when they said to me, let us go to the house of the LORD.' The Romanesque architecture, with its massive stone walls and rhythmic arches, speaks of the Church as fortress and refuge — 'God is our refuge and strength' (Ps 46:1). The building itself is an act of theology in stone, its Romanesque solidity expressing the unchanging nature of God and his Word. Le Puy was also a center of Marian devotion, housing a venerated Black Madonna, connecting it to the ancient tradition of venerating the Mother of God as the one through whom salvation entered the world.

scripture_refs.reason: I was glad when they said let us go to the house of the LORD — the pilgrims ascending the cathedral steps
God is our refuge and strength — the fortress-like Romanesque architecture embodies divine protection
How lovely is your dwelling place — the beauty of the sacred architecture glorifying God
